records.content:
During this project, we first explored several datasets as a group and selected those related to education and lifestyle. These datasets allowed us to examine how different lifestyle factors may influence educational performance. Based on this, we aimed to answer the question of whether a strict lifestyle or a more balanced lifestyle leads to better academic outcomes. To investigate both perspectives, we created eight visualizations for each side, presenting evidence that either supports or challenges the effectiveness of each lifestyle.

Our findings suggest that a stricter lifestyle is generally associated with better educational performance. However, the data also indicates that this approach may lead to higher levels of stress. This highlights a trade-off between academic success and well-being, suggesting that while strict routines can improve performance, they may come at a personal cost.

We chose four datasets from Kaggle, which all include variables about lifestyle and education.

Dataset 1: Student lifestyle and academic performance (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/steve1215rogg​/student​-lifestyle​-dataset)

Dataset 2: Gaming dataset (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/aiexplorer77​/gaming​-vs​-academic​-performance)

Dataset 3: Alcohol consumption dataset (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/uciml​/student​-alcohol​-consumption).

Dataset 4: Student Habits vs Academic Performance (

https://​www​.kaggle​.com​/datasets​/jayaantanaath​/student​-habits​-vs​-academic​-performance​/data)

Variables used in the data story are listed and categorized below.
Dataset 1 (D1) - Student lifestyle and academic performance:

Stress level (ordinal): Level of stress reported in 3 levels - low, moderate, high

GPA (ratio): Average grade on a 4.0 max scale

Dataset 2 (D2) - Gaming vs Academic Performance:

Gaming hours (ratio): Average gaming hours per day

Reaction time (ratio): Average response time

Grades (ratio): Final grade score from 0-100 scale

Grade category (nominal): classifies students’ performance into three ordered levels: fail, sufficient, and good.

Dataset 3 (D3) - Student Alcohol Consumption:

Study time (ordinal): Weekly study time in four categories (1 = <2 hours, 2 = 2-5 hours, 3 = 5-10 hours, 4 = > 10 hours)

Final grade(ratio): Final grade (G3) on 0-20 scale

Weekday alcohol consumption(ordinal): Alcohol consumption during weekdays rated from very low (1) to very high (5)

Weekend alcohol consumption (ordinal): Alcohol consumption during weekends rated from very low (1) to very high (5)

Absences (ratio): Number of school absences

Paid extra classes (nominal): Whether the student attended paid extra educational support (Yes/No).

Course (nominal) whether the observation is for math or Portuguese classes, these are separate datasets.

Dataset 4 (D4) - Student Habits vs Academic Performance:

Study hours per day (ratio): Average daily study time.

Exam score (ratio): Final examination score.

To preprocess datasets above, we first inspect the descriptive details of each dataset and remove irrelevant variables. Then, we checked for missing values and duplicate records which we removed. To handle missing values, we used median imputation for numerical variables and mode imputation for categorical variables. Besides, we also check for outliers. Lastly, we removed illogical datapoints, for example those who have grades above 100 (the maximum of grading scale used) in dataset 3.

Specific preprocessing attempts in perspective 1

In dataset 1, we encoded students’ grade variables into categorical variables called Grade Group using percentile tertile grouping (pandas.qcut), which divides students’ grade into three approximately equal-sized groups: Low, Medium, High.

Specific preprocessing attempts in perspective 2

In dataset 3, we generalised the grades from 1-20 to 1-10. Also, we decided to make a new column called ‘Alcohol’ to generalise alcohol consumption over the entire week by adding the columns workday alcohol (Walc) and weekend alcohol (Dalc) together and dividing the new number by two, so we remain with a 1 to 5 scale.

\text{Normalized Study Time}
=
\frac{x-x_{\min}}{x_{\max}-x_{\min}}\times10

The x is the original study time,  x_{\min}  and  x_{\max}  are the minimum and maximum study time values within each dataset. This allows the comparison between datasets in study time to become meaningful and relative.
For example: In Dataset 4, study time ranges from 0 to 8.3 study hours per day. A student who studies 6 hours is rescaled as:\frac{6.0 - 0}{8.3 - 0} \times 10 = 7.23

Then, a study time of 6 is represented as 7.23 on the 0-10 scale.

What factors help improve academic performance has always been a crucial question of generations of students and also researchers in the education field (e.g. 

Adelantado-Renau et al. (2019), 

Singh et al. (2018), 

Camacho-Morles et al. (2021)).

In some perspectives, a successful study method is investing most of study time per day without any distraction from social activities, gaming or entertainment. Previous study shows that having serious learning behaviors for everyday plays an important role in grades (

Credé & Kuncel (2008)). However, student life is not just studying. They also spent time socializing and participating in other physical or extracurricular activities. Studying in a strict way might not be the most effective method to acquire high scores. Studies found that sports participation and extracurricular activities may also benefit students’ academic achievement (

Barbosa et al. (2020),  

Gilman et al. (2003)).

Therefore, this data story aims to investigate whether living chill or strictly would be more beneficial to students’ study, and the role of external variables such as study hours and gaming in the relationship between type of lifestyle and academic performance. Specifically, two central perspectives in this story are “Having a strict lifestyle (e.g. only focusing on studying and not participating in leisure activities) improves students’ academic performance.” and “Having a chill lifestyle (e.g. combining studying and participating in leisure activities) improves students’ academic performance.”
Strict lifestyle means focusing more on studying and reducing leisure activities. In this part, we discuss how strict lifestyle affects academic performance.

Students spending more time studying tend to achieve higher academic performance.

%run ../plots/study_time_plot.ipynb



Figure 1. Average Study Time by Grade Group Across Four Datasets

Study time was chosen as the first indicator of strict lifestyle because it shows the students’ investment and prioritization of learning. Across four datasets, higher study time is consistently correlated with higher academic performance. However, the relationship is not similar across subjects, as shown in dataset 3. The reason is because of different learning demands of two subjects, as mathematics often requires more structured problem-solving tactics, while language relies heavily on continuous exposure, self-practicing and application in reality (

Foster (2023), 

Zhang & Zou (2020)). However, study time does not determine academic success, it also depends on learning strategies, quality, attention, and motivation of students (

Rosário et al. (2012)).

Students with fewer absences tend to achieve higher academic performance.

%run ../plots/attendance_plot.ipynb



Figure 2. Average Absences by Grade Group in Mathematics and Portuguese

Absence was selected as another indicator of strict lifestyle, as it reflects students’ academic discipline. In both Mathematics and Portuguese, students in high grade group show fewest average absences, while medium and low grade groups are absent more frequently. This is supported by 

Credé et al. (2010) that absenteeism can lead to lower grades, as missing classes reduces learning opportunities and classroom engagement.

Students who get higher grades tend to combine intensive study time with extra classes

%run ../plots/extra_class_plot.ipynb



Figure 3. Average Final Grade by Study Time and Paid Extra Classes

Extra classes were included as another indicator of strict lifestyle because it reports students’ additional investment beyond mandatory classes. However, this pattern differs by subject. For Mathematics, students attending extra classes showed higher grades as study time increased. This is because extra classes or study time may not fully explain mathematical grades; their performance may also depend on learning strategies and conceptual understanding (

Uyandıran & Tarım (2024), 

TURGUT (2021)). For Portuguese, students studying more without extra classes tend to achieve higher grades than those who combine both of them, while the latter showed a fluctuating pattern. This difference lies in the subject characteristics: mathematics requires teacher’s problem-solving methods and guidance (

Alegre-Ansuategui et al. (2017), 

Uyandıran & Tarım (2024)), while with foreign languages, self-study and practice in reality will be more effective in improving students’ language skills (

Nakata (2016), 

Zhang & Zou (2022)).

Despite possibly affecting mental wellbeing, strictly follow intense study schedule brings fruitful result

%run ../plots/pers1_subarg4.ipynb





Figure 4 Grade Distribution by Stress Level

Overall, data shows that high stress correlates with high grades. Since strict, intensive study schedules are a very plausible reason students report high stress (rather than e.g. financial stress or personal issues, given this is a student lifestyle dataset), this chart visually supports the idea that strict lifestyles pay off academically. This phenomenon can be explained by the concept of eustress, which is a type of positive stress that result in positive outcome, which stems from the Yerkes-Dodson Law stating that ‘‘increasing stress is beneficial to performance until some optimum level is reached…’’ (

Le Fevre et al. (2003)) However, it is worth noting that grades spread quite widely in the high stress group, which is shown more clearly in the with-datapoints version, thus the pattern may not apply universally every time.
The balanced lifestyle is about combining studying and leisure activities. In this part we will show how alcohol consumption and gaming impact education performance.

The first argument that we use for the balanced lifestyle perspective is that alcohol consumption has a negative impact on the final grade of a student, this argument is based on the paper of 

Carrell et al. (2011). The paper found that increased alcohol consumption caused significant reductions in student achievement, particularly among high-performing students.

%run ../plots/alcohol_consumption_plot1.ipynb



Figure 5: Distribution of alcohol consumption

%run ../plots/alcohol_consumption_plot.ipynb



Figure 6: Final grade by average alcohol consumption

The second graph shows a weak negative correlation between final grades, average alcohol consumption, and course. A slight decline in performance can be observed as alcohol consumption increases, with a somewhat stronger effect for Portuguese than for Mathematics. The first graph also shows that alcohol consumption levels between 4 and 5 are underrepresented, which may influence these findings. Overall, our results partially support the findings of 

Carrell et al. (2011), who also found that increased alcohol consumption was associated with lower grades, although the relationship in our data appears less pronounced.

The second argument that we bring for the balanced lifestyle perspective is that spending more time gaming will reduce grades, but may improve response times which in turn will improve eduaction performance. This is based on the research of 

Prabu Kumar et al. (2020), who found that higher response times improve cognitive performance. 

Chandra et al. (2016) proved that gaming improves response times. This means that gaming would improve

%run ../plots/gaming_stats_3d.ipynb



Figure 7: Reaction time vs Grades vs Gaming Hours

%run ../plots/gaming_response_time.ipynb



Figure 8: How reaction time influences grades

The two figures suggest that reaction time does not appear to have a strong influence on grades, because the data points do not show a clear or consistent pattern. In contrast, the amount of gaming seems to be more strongly related to grades, since the trend in that graph is more noticeable. Taken together, this means our results do not support the idea that reaction time is an important predictor of grades in this dataset. That is interesting because it seems to contradict 

Prabu Kumar et al. (2020), who reports a different relationship between these factors and academic performance. The paper suggests that reaction time has a positive impact on educational performance because faster reaction times may reflect quicker information processing, better attentional control, or more efficient cognitive functioning, which can support learning and test performance.

We used bar charts to compare the average study time across low, medium, and high grade groups. Four datasets were included to show consistent patterns of the data that high grade groups show more investment on their study time, not to compare between datasets from different schools. To improve comparability, study time was rescaled into the same 0-10 scale, clearly showing how students’ study time within the same time frame affected their scores. In the Student Alcohol Consumption dataset, the dataset was appended into a single table, with new column called “Subject” as the legend to distinguish two subjects.

We used grouped bar charts to compare the average absent number of Mathematics and Portuguese students across grade groups. The x-axis indicates the students’ grade group, while y-axis shows the average absences. The legend was used to encode two subjects, helping audience distinguish whether the absence pattern differs between two subjects.

A line chart was used to illustrate how the relationship between combined study time and average final grade differs depending on whether students attended extra classes or not. Study time is shown on the x-axis, average final grade is represented as the y-axis, and legend color distinguishes students who attended extra classes and those who did not attend, making differences in trend to easily compare.

We believe median is the most suitable statistic to represent the typical performance of the groups because the size of different stress groups are not equal. Extreme values are more likely to occur by chance in larger groups and dragging the mean around. From that, boxplot is chosen as it not only displays the median effectively but also shows the distribution of students’ grades in each group. Two versions of graphs (with and without datapoints) are available for readers who want to inspect the spread of outliers in detail. Contrast of colors is utilized: darker shade of red delivers an increasing level of stress.

Here a histogram is used as the main mark to compare alcohol consumption levels and percentages. Position, height, and color encode the values, with separate colors for the Mathematics and Portuguese datasets. The data includes ordinal, nominal, and ratio variables, which makes the bar chart a suitable choice for clear category comparison.

Figure 6 uses boxplots to show the distribution of final grades across alcohol consumption levels. Position, size, and color are used to display the values and distinguish the two datasets. The plot also includes outliers, median values, and spread, giving a fuller view of how grades vary across categories.

Figure 7 shows the relationship between grades, reaction time, and gaming hours in a 3D scatter plot, with each axis clearly labeled and a note that lower reaction times indicate faster responses. The points are colored by grade category using a traffic-light palette, and only 500 rows are sampled to keep the figure easier to read.

Figure 8 expands on figure 7 by using a 2D scatter plot of grades versus reaction time, with point size representing gaming hours. It also adds violin plots to show how gaming hours are distributed across grade categories and boxplots to compare grades more directly. The same color scheme is reused so the figures stay visually consistent.

On June 23th 2026, our group presented the story draft to the class and received some peer feedback, which mainly focused on strengthening the interpretation of our visualizations and showing the distinct comparisons across datasets more clearly:

Normalize the study time into the same scale in figure 1.

Focus the study discipline explanation on Dataset 3 (absence level), as the other datasets show similar attendance percentages across groups.

Clarify and emphasize the justification for stress level in the strict lifestyle.

Simplify the alcohol consumption intensity by reducing the repeated patterns in the bar chart.

Remove the boxplot from grades versus reaction time with a gaming scatter plot.

Following the feedback, our team reviewed the work carefully and made changes accordingly.

All members found the datasets, did preprocessing and wrote reflection by themselves.
Ian handled technical tasks such as Github organizing and website building. Ian wrote conclusion, he and David also created graphs and wrote arguments and reflection in perspective 2. Mai Anh wrote introduction, Ngoc wrote peer feedback, and they created graphs and arguments for perspective 1.
